subtopics.title: Definition and Importance of Commerce
common_misconceptions: Commerce is the same as business
Commerce only involves buying and selling
Industry and commerce are separate unrelated activities
subtopics.teaching_note: Commerce = all activities that facilitate the distribution and exchange of goods and services. Scope: home trade (retail, wholesale), foreign trade (import, export), aids to trade (banking, insurance, transport, warehousing, advertising, communication). Industry creates form utility; commerce creates place, time, and possession utility.

subtopics.title: Divisions of Commerce
common_misconceptions: Aids to trade are not part of commerce
Home trade only means trade within a house
All foreign trade is import trade
subtopics.teaching_note: Divisions: 1) Trade - buying and selling of goods (home trade: wholesale, retail; foreign trade: import, export). 2) Aids to trade - services that make trade possible (banking, insurance, transport, warehousing, advertising, communication/tourism). Nigerian context: NCS (Nigeria Customs Service) regulates foreign trade, NAFDAC regulates imports of food/drugs.

subtopics.title: Home Trade
common_misconceptions: Wholesalers only sell to retailers (they can sell to manufacturers too)
Retailers don't provide any valuable service
Chain stores and multiple shops are different
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Alaba International Market (Lagos) - major wholesale electronics market. Onitsha Main Market - one of West Africa's largest markets. Modern retail: Shoprite, Spar, Market Square. Small-scale retailers dominate Nigerian trade: kiosks, roadside sellers, market traders. Functions of wholesalers: bulk buying, storage, financing retailers, breaking bulk, risk bearing.

subtopics.title: Foreign Trade
common_misconceptions: Balance of trade = balance of payments (BOT is only goods, BOP includes services and capital)
Favourable balance is always good (can indicate under-consumption)
Tariffs always reduce imports (they may not if demand is inelastic)
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Major exports: crude oil, cocoa, cashew, sesame seeds, urea. Major imports: refined petroleum, vehicles, machinery, chemicals. BOT = exports - imports (goods only). BOP = BOT + invisible trade + capital transfers. Nigeria typically has BOT surplus (oil exports) but BOP issues due to capital flight. Trade protection: tariffs, quotas, embargoes, subsidies.

subtopics.title: Transportation
common_misconceptions: The fastest transport is always the best
Water transport is only for coastal areas
Pipeline transport carries all types of goods
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Road transport dominates (90%+ of freight and passengers). Challenges: poor road conditions (Lagos-Ibadan expressway, Benin-Ore road). Rail: revitalization with Chinese loans (Abuja-Kaduna standard gauge). Air: MMA (Lagos), Nnamdi Azikiwe (Abuja), Port Harcourt. Water: Lagos ports (Apapa, Tin Can), Onne, Calabar. Pipeline: crude oil and petroleum products (vandalism is a major issue).

subtopics.title: Banking and Insurance
common_misconceptions: Insurance is gambling (it's risk transfer)
Life insurance is a contract of indemnity (NO - it's valued policy)
All risks are insurable
subtopics.teaching_note: Principles of insurance: Uberrimae fidei (utmost good faith), insurable interest (must have financial stake), indemnity (compensate for loss, not profit), contribution (multiple insurers share claim), subrogation (insurer takes over insured's rights), proximate cause. Types: life assurance, marine, fire, motor, accident. Nigerian insurers: AIICO, Custodian, Leadway, LASACO. NAICOM regulates.

subtopics.title: Forms of Business Ownership
common_misconceptions: Private company cannot be listed on stock exchange (correct, but some are confused)
All companies must have PLC or Ltd in their name
Cooperatives are government-owned
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Registration at CAC. Most Nigerian businesses are sole traders (market traders, artisans). Partnerships common among professionals (lawyers, accountants). Major PLCs: Dangote Cement, MTN Nigeria, Guaranty Trust Bank, Zenith Bank. Public corporations: NNPC, NPA, NTA, FAAN. Privatization: started with SAP (1986), continued with Bureau of Public Enterprises. Dangote Group started as trading firm, now Africa's largest cement producer.

subtopics.title: Advertising
common_misconceptions: Advertising and personal selling are the same
All advertising is deceptive
Advertising only benefits manufacturers
subtopics.teaching_note: Advertising = paid, non-personal communication to promote ideas, goods, or services. Types: informative (new product info), persuasive (brand switching), reminder (keep brand in mind). Media: TV (NTA, Channels, AIT), radio, newspapers (Punch, Vanguard, Guardian), billboards, social media, cinema. APCON regulates advertising in Nigeria. APCON was established by Act 55 of 1988.
